model.label: Claude Sonnet 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Fast flagship Claude model for coding, analysis, and high-throughput agent use.

pick.rationale: Iowa State enters this Sweet 16 as the analytically superior team, ranking 7th nationally in KenPom with an elite defense ranked 6th-8th nationally (DRtg 95.6) and an offense ranked 15th — meaningfully outpacing Virginia's KenPom #13 profile on both ends of the floor. Both teams carry significant injuries: Virginia loses De'Andre Hunter (ACC Sixth Man of the Year, broken wrist, out for the full tournament), a severe blow to their offensive depth, while Iowa State loses Keshon Gilbert but retains Tamin Lipsey, Joshua Jefferson (16.4 PPG, 7.4 RPG), and Milan Momcilovic as a three-headed scoring attack. Iowa State's turnover-forcing defensive system — averaging 19.4 points off turnovers per game — is a particularly dangerous matchup for a Virginia offense now lacking Hunter's versatility, and the Cyclones' Big 12 battle-testing and scheme-based defensive identity give them a clear edge despite Ugonna Onyenso's elite interior presence for the Cavaliers.

reasoningStep.title: Virginia vs Iowa State Sweet 16 — Efficiency, Injuries, and Matchup Context
reasoningStep.summary: Iowa State holds a meaningful efficiency edge over Virginia at KenPom (#7 vs. #13) and Torvik, with a superior defense (6th-8th nationally) and offense (15th nationally). Virginia loses De'Andre Hunter (ACC Sixth Man of the Year, broken wrist) for the full tournament, while Iowa State loses Keshon Gilbert but retains Lipsey, Jefferson, and Momcilovic. Iowa State's turnover-forcing system and three-headed scoring attack give them a credible path past Virginia's elite interior defense, and expert consensus identifies the Cyclones as a legitimate championship contender.
evidence: Virginia KenPom #13, AdjEM +26.49, AdjOE 122.2 (28th nationally), AdjDE 95.7 (14th nationally by Torvik)
Iowa State KenPom #7, AdjOE ranked 15th nationally (124.5), AdjDE ranked 6th-8th nationally (DRtg 95.6), SRS +27.15
Virginia: De'Andre Hunter (ACC Sixth Man of the Year) OUT for full tournament — broken left wrist suffered in ACC Tournament
Iowa State: Keshon Gilbert OUT for tournament (groin); Tamin Lipsey returned for R64; Jefferson (16.4 PPG, 7.4 RPG) and Momcilovic are core contributors
Iowa State forces 15+ turnovers per game, averages 19.4 points off turnovers — a severe matchup problem for Virginia's offense
ESPN bracket analysis flagged Iowa State going through Virginia's Ugonna Onyenso as the key Sweet 16 challenge
SI analysis: 'Iowa State has been one of the best teams in the country all season and are a legitimate threat to win the national championship'
Iowa State is the higher seed (2 vs. 3) and holds a consistent efficiency advantage across all major rating systems

result.text: 2026 March Madness Bracket Predictions – Nate Silver Subscribe Sign in Models & Forecasts 2026 March Madness Predictions Silver Bulletin odds for the men's NCAA tournament, powered by our new COOPER ratings. Nate Silver , Joseph George , and Eli McKown-Dawson Mar 17, 2026 ∙ Paid 98 26 12 Share See also: Women’s March Madness Predictions , Men’s COOPER ratings . 🏀 The latest March Madness projections Updated March 17, 2026 As of ~6:15PM, we’ve updated the projections with fresh injury information. Since the First Four games will tip soon — I know some people don’t consider them part of the tournament, but we do — you should consider this version our “official” pre-tournament forecast. There are no major changes, but we now have a more pessimistic timeline for any return of Duke’s Caleb Foster. We also toned down the effect of injuries in an update last night. 1 Even small differences can compound over six rounds, and as a result, Michigan and Duke, which have more injury problems and were therefore more affected by injury handling, are now basically tied with Arizona as tourney favorites. We’ve set up the spreadsheet to include an archive of all previous predictions so you can see the effect of these updates. We’ll continue to update the projections as games are completed . The text below will also change as our forecasts do. -NS , 3/17/26 Sure, it might seem like the world’s gone a little crazy. But at least we’ll be getting a dose of March Madness. The NCAA tournament is perhaps our very favorite event here at Silver Bulletin. 2 In fact, the tournament really needs no introduction. I’ve been running versions of these projections since 2011, almost ten years before I’d even heard of Substack. But pretty much every part of the model has changed at some point . Instead of compiling other people’s projections, we now mostly rely on our own in the form of our new COOPER ratings — although we blend them with Ken Pomeroy’s ratings (because we think Pomeroy is pretty much the best, present company excluded.) We’ve also upgraded our technology stack. Believe it or not, until last year, part of the work for our NCAA model was still being done in Microsoft Excel. 3 Now we’ve built the code to run proper simulations — 100,000 at a time — which enables more sophisticated handling of injuries (and there are a lot of them this year), among other things. This isn’t an “AI-driven” model quite yet, though Claude and other AI tools are increasingly helping us smooth out some of the code's rough patches and turn these numbers around more quickly. Still, March Madness is eternal. If nothing else, there will be upsets: we’ve calculated that the odds of having a perfect bracket are on the order of 1 in 10 quintillion. I guess I’ll just have to concede that we’re living in a simulation if it happens. How our NCAA forecasts work This is what we call a landing page, meaning that both the numbers and the text will change. We strongly recommend checking out the web versio

result.text: 2026 March Madness Bracket Predictions – Nate Silver Subscribe Sign in Models & Forecasts 2026 March Madness Predictions Silver Bulletin odds for the men's NCAA tournament, powered by our new COOPER ratings. Nate Silver , Joseph George , and Eli McKown-Dawson Mar 17, 2026 ∙ Paid 98 26 12 Share See also: Women’s March Madness Predictions , Men’s COOPER ratings . 🏀 The latest March Madness projections Updated March 17, 2026 As of ~6:15PM, we’ve updated the projections with fresh injury information. Since the First Four games will tip soon — I know some people don’t consider them part of the tournament, but we do — you should consider this version our “official” pre-tournament forecast. There are no major changes, but we now have a more pessimistic timeline for any return of Duke’s Caleb Foster. We also toned down the effect of injuries in an update last night. 1 Even small differences can compound over six rounds, and as a result, Michigan and Duke, which have more injury problems and were therefore more affected by injury handling, are now basically tied with Arizona as tourney favorites. We’ve set up the spreadsheet to include an archive of all previous predictions so you can see the effect of these updates. We’ll continue to update the projections as games are completed . The text below will also change as our forecasts do. -NS , 3/17/26 Sure, it might seem like the world’s gone a little crazy. But at least we’ll be getting a dose of March Madness. The NCAA tournament is perhaps our very favorite event here at Silver Bulletin. 2 In fact, the tournament really needs no introduction. I’ve been running versions of these projections since 2011, almost ten years before I’d even heard of Substack. But pretty much every part of the model has changed at some point . Instead of compiling other people’s projections, we now mostly rely on our own in the form of our new COOPER ratings — although we blend them with Ken Pomeroy’s ratings (because we think Pomeroy is pretty much the best, present company excluded.) We’ve also upgraded our technology stack. Believe it or not, until last year, part of the work for our NCAA model was still being done in Microsoft Excel. 3 Now we’ve built the code to run proper simulations — 100,000 at a time — which enables more sophisticated handling of injuries (and there are a lot of them this year), among other things. This isn’t an “AI-driven” model quite yet, though Claude and other AI tools are increasingly helping us smooth out some of the code's rough patches and turn these numbers around more quickly. Still, March Madness is eternal. If nothing else, there will be upsets: we’ve calculated that the odds of having a perfect bracket are on the order of 1 in 10 quintillion. I guess I’ll just have to concede that we’re living in a simulation if it happens. How our NCAA forecasts work This is what we call a landing page, meaning that both the numbers and the text will change. We strongly recommend checking out the web versio

**Key findings:**

**Virginia (3-seed, KenPom #13, AdjEM +26.49, Torvik #14):**
- 29-4 record, ACC Tournament runners-up
- Elite defense (AdjDE 95.7, 14th nationally by Torvik)
- Offense ranked 28th nationally (AdjOE 122.2)
- **Critical injury: De'Andre Hunter (ACC Sixth Man of the Year, broken wrist) — OUT for entire tournament**
- Ugonna Onyenso is a dominant interior presence (ACC Tournament record 21 blocks)
- Ryan Odom's first year, excellent late-season form

**Iowa State (2-seed, KenPom #7, AdjEM ~+27-28, AdjOE 15th, AdjDE 8th nationally):**
- 27-7 record, Big 12 battle-tested
- Elite defense ranked 6th-8th nationally (DRtg 95.6)
- **Keshon Gilbert OUT for tournament (groin)** — 2nd leading scorer
- **Tamin Lipsey returning** (optimistic vs. Lipscomb R64) — All-Big 12 2nd team, All-Defensive team
- Milan Momcilovic and Joshua Jefferson (16.4 PPG, 7.4 RPG) are the core
- Three-headed attack forces 15+ turnovers per game, averages 19.4 points off turnovers
- T.J. Otzelberger's defensive system is elite and scheme-based

**Matchup analysis:**
- Iowa State is actually ranked **higher** than Virginia in KenPom (#7 vs. #13) and has a superior AdjDE (8th vs. 14th nationally)
- Both teams have significant injuries: Virginia loses Hunter (Sixth Man of the Year), Iowa State loses Gilbert (2nd leading scorer) but retains Lipsey
- Iowa State's turnover-forcing defense is a severe matchup problem for Virginia's offense
- Virginia's Onyenso is a legitimate interior challenge for Iowa State, but Iowa State's three-headed scoring attack (Jefferson, Lipsey, Momcilovic) provides multiple answers
- Iowa State is the higher seed (2 vs. 3) and the analytically superior team
- Expert consensus (ESPN, SI) projects Iowa State as a legitimate Final Four/championship contender
